author: @st0rmerc00l8
text:
Personally I'm not a fan of things like ratchets for most things as they wear down, and prevent backdriving.

I mentioned previously about using a variable pantograph with the prior reciprocating rack mechanism (the one that used partial gears), but instead of a pantograph, a bar with a pivot at the center, and each end attached to a reciprocating input and output, where the input can change its distance from the pivot point, would allow the cvt mechanism to work without ratchets and be backdrivable.

replies.author: @st0rmerc00l8
replies.text: When I get my hands on a 3d printer, I'll make this mechanism.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - but the issue is converting the reciprocating motion back into rotation - that's hard to do when the stroke length is variable - can I suggest you design one make it and show us all what you mean - oh by the way I have already done a couple of lever versions but the issue I mention remains

Does it work the other way too?

replies.author: @GabrielPettier
replies.text:
@trollmcclure1884 Half of mechanics is turning circular motions into linear ones or linear into circular ;). This one wouldn't know where to stop and switch gear, but there are plenty of ways to achieve that, if you fetch the PDF Robert mentions, 889 and 894 are such devices (around page 230), for example.
What's neat about The device in this video, is that it allows controlling the speed of the rotation by the range of motion.

author: @SAILBOATJACARANDA
text: Cool stuff bud. Here's an idea: Was thinking about how one with minimal tools can utilise freecad. I think using a cheapo mini projector to project designs onto sheet material for cutting could be really powerful for those unable to afford CNC kit. Might be worth a vid or two.

replies.author: @eh1702
replies.text: A lot of people use these little projectors (often really cheap, almost obsolete ones or bought secondhand) to cut out sewing patterns straight from the fabric — ie. Project designs onto sheet material for cutting. So you can see here on youtube how easy it is, and what details need troubleshooting.

replies.author: @eh1702
replies.text: By the way, you can also use “tracing” software bought for a the price of a couple of cups of coffee (like camera ludica) to project from your phone or tablet.

replies.author: @eh1702
replies.text: I mean it does not direct project but allows you to see in the screen the image superimposed so that you can draw it.  It can do a surprisingly large range of scales.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Cool idea mate - accuracy might be an issue but maybe some kind measurement line on the work piece that you line up with the projection?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice posts mate - very helpful thanks for sharing
